main_turf.bias_notes: Counter-Clockwise. A relatively flat country track with an incredibly short 250m home straight. Because the final stretch is so brief, the track naturally creates a massive bias toward front-runners ('E' types) and horses that can race prominently on the pace. Closers ('Swoopers') must begin their runs well before the home turn, forcing them wide.
main_turf.post_bias: Inside barriers (1-7) hold a severe statistical advantage, particularly in the 1100m and 1200m sprint events. Horses drawn wide (barrier 9+) face a significant disadvantage, as they either burn too much energy crossing over or are forced to cover extra ground on the turns.

handicapping_angles.the_short_straight_trap: Aggressively downgrade deep closers. With only 250 metres of straight runway, horses settling in the back half of the field will almost always run out of ground, even if a pace duel occurs up front.
handicapping_angles.the_gippsland_heavy: Like many tracks in the Gippsland region, Moe frequently races on deeply rain-affected surfaces (Heavy 8 to Heavy 10) during the Victorian winter. Heavily upgrade proven 'mudlarks' who have documented success on genuinely heavy going, as winter races here often become a slog.
handicapping_angles.barrier_bias_penalty: Apply a strict speed-figure penalty to horses drawn wide (outside barrier 9) in sprint races. The ground loss on this tight track layout is highly detrimental to finishing positions.
